heading: libro del trattato dedicato ad Almansore38, e anche Galeno39, Avicenna40, Alberto Magno41, Michele Scoto42, il Conciliatore43 e molti nostri
card_summary: This chunk contains the heart of the Tono Sesto (Sixth Tone) of Zorzi's Cantico Primo, comprising thirty chapters (Caput V–XXXIV) on the human being as microcosm. Zorzi demonstrates that man contains within himself — in hierarchical analogy — all levels of created being: the four elements, stones, metals, mixed minerals, plants, animals, the seven planetary spheres, the sphere of the fixed stars, the angels, and ultimately God himself. His sources range from the Kabbalistic etymology of Adam (alef = divinity, dalet = celestial nature, mem = corruptible matter) and the Hebrew urim ve-tummim, through Hermes Trismegistus, Zoroaster, Pythagoras, Plato, Aristotle, Boethius, and Origen, to Avicenna, Galen, Hippocrates, Albert the Great, Pietro d'Abano, Empedocles, Porphyry, Iamblichus, Proclus, and Augustine. Scriptural anchors include Genesis 2:7 (spiraculum vitarum), Psalms, Leviticus, Isaiah, Ezekiel, Malachias, Apocalypse, and the Gospel of John. The cosmological argument culminates in correspondences between specific human faculties and the seven planets, then between the whole man and the angels, and finally in a treatment of the "membra Dei" — the divine members — as an image of the Trinity in which man participates.
essay:
Now I'll expand this essay from approximately 1200 words to 2000-4000 words by adding deeper scholarly analysis, more detailed engagement with sources, fuller treatment of theological implications, and comprehensive explanations of referenced concepts. I'll preserve all blockquotes, citations, and formatting exactly as they appear.

# Zorzi, *De Harmonia Mundi* — Tono Sesto, Capp. V–XXXIV: The Human Being as Universal Microcosm

**Note:** This summary covers the opening portion of this very long section (approx. the first 600–800 lines out of ~3,600), supplemented by chapter headings and spot-checks of later chapters. The full section runs from Caput V to Caput XXXIV.

---

## Introduction: Physiognomy and the Context of the Section

The chunk opens mid-argument within a discussion of physiognomy and the reading of character from physical signs — attributed to Pythagoras, to Fraotes king of India (via Apollonius of Tyana), to Julius Caesar and Sulla, and supported by a citation from Job ("Ha inciso sul palmo della mano degli uomini"). Zorzi notes that physicians read bodily constitution from the colour of the face, offering an elaborate Galenic-Avicennian scheme of the four humours and their chromatic signatures. This opening serves as the porch to the main edifice: the systematic demonstration that the whole cosmos is mirrored in man.

The physiognomic frame is not merely ornamental. Zorzi is invoking one of the most ancient philosophical traditions in the Mediterranean world — the belief that the external and material form encodes the internal and spiritual essence. Pythagoras's reputation as a physiognomist connects to his broader teaching on the soul's transmigration and its enclosure in the body; Julius Caesar's and Sulla's use of physiognomy to read men's characters follows a Stoic premise that virtue and vice leave their marks on the flesh. Job's reference to the divine inscription upon the human palm (understood by Zorzi and his contemporaries as the divine image impressed upon bodily form) grounds this empirical observation in theological warrant. The four humours themselves — blood, phlegm, yellow bile, black bile — are not inert substances but vehicles of psychic and pneumatic forces. Their colours (blood's redness, phlegm's whiteness, bile's yellow and dark hues) become indices of the soul's temperament. Thus physiognomy opens directly into cosmology: the body is a text, and that text is legible because the cosmos itself is written in the same alphabet.

---

## Caput V: "Tutto è compreso nell'uomo: discorso generale" (Everything is contained in man: a general discourse)

The central thesis of the entire section is announced: the "sommo Artefice" has constructed man as a recapitulation of all reality. Zorzi's proof-text is Genesis 2:7 — "Formavit Dominus Deus hominem pulverem de terra, et inspiravit in faciem eius spiraculum vitarum" — in which the two extremes of all existence (earth and divine life) are simultaneously named, with everything intermediate implicitly included.

> *"Infimum enim et prae omnibus minoratam est terrae elementum, supremum vero ille, qui ait: Ego sum vita, in omnia videlicet vitae radios diffundens. Et ut omnes vitae gradus in homine conclusos declararet Moses, non ab re dixit spiraculum vitarum, quia omnibus vitae gradibus vivit hic homo."*
<cite>p. 602 (Latin text, Caput V)</cite>

This passage is the architectural foundation of Zorzi's entire anthropology. The divine Name "Ego sum vita" (I am life) — the Tetragrammaton's proclamation in Exodus 3:14 refracted through Johannine theology — represents not abstract being but dynamic vitality. "Spiraculum vitarum" (breath of lives, plural) is Zorzi's precise rendering of the Hebrew nishmät chayim (נִשְׁמַת חַיִּים). The plural "lives" is critical: it indicates not a single, monolithic principle animating the human being, but a graduated hierarchy of vital principles, each distinct yet harmonised in a single form. Thus Genesis 2:7 becomes the scriptural warrant for what Aristotelian philosophy calls the "hierarchy of souls" (vegetative, sensitive, rational) — but Zorzi deepens this by insisting that the divine breath itself comprises all intermediate grades, collapsing any separation between natural philosophy and pneumatology.

Zorzi then provides a Kabbalistic etymology of the Hebrew name Adam (אדם): alef (א) = divinity; dalet (ד) = celestial nature; mem (מ) = the corporeal and corruptible — thus encoding in man's very name the triad of divine, celestial, and material principles. This etymological method belongs to the hermeneutical tradition of Merkabah mysticism and later Kabbalism, in which the Hebrew letters themselves bear ontological weight. The name is not arbitrary designation but revelation of essence; to speak Adam's name is to invoke the vertical architecture of creation compressed into three letters. This technique of **notarikon** (acronymic reading) and **gematria** (numerical correspondence) was inherited by Renaissance Christian Kabbalah (especially through Pico della Mirandola and Johannes Reuchlin) as a method of deciphering the divine speech-acts embedded in the Torah. Zorzi's deployment of it here establishes that the human microcosm is not merely a philosophical abstraction but a theurgic reality encoded in the very language God chose to reveal.

Man is said to live simultaneously the life of stones and elements (mere being and alterability), of metals (a hidden vital spirit), of plants (vegetative life), of animals (sensation and motion), of separated intelligences (rational life), and of God himself (divine and eternal life). This graduated vitalism draws on Hermes Trismegistus ("L'uomo è una totalità e un insieme compatto nel tutto"), on Augustine (the soul as "omnium rerum similitudo"), and on Boethius, who describes the harmony of the rational soul in musical terms — grave and acute voices resolved in one consonance.

The invocation of Hermes Trismegistus here is crucial for understanding the Hermetic strand in Zorzi's thought. The *Pimander* (the opening treatise of the *Corpus Hermeticum*) contains the teaching that humanity is created "in the image and likeness" of the Divine Mind (Nous), and that the human being alone among earthly creatures participates in the divine nature through the intellect. Zorzi is reading the Genesis 1:27 imago Dei through a Hermetic lens: the image is not merely a moral or intellectual resemblance but an ontological correspondence. The phrase "totalità e un insieme compatto nel tutto" (a totality and a compact whole in the All) echoes the Hermetic principle of sympatheia, the cosmic sympathy or resonance through which all parts of the universe are knit together in living correspondence.

Augustine's formulation of the soul as "similitudo omnium rerum" (resemblance of all things) appears in his commentary on the Psalms and in the *De Quantitate Animae*. For Augustine, the soul's universality stems not from material composition but from its rational faculty's capacity to cognise all things through the intellect. This is the Christian Neoplatonic heritage: the soul, as a created image of the eternal Forms, carries within its intelligible light a reflected radiance of the entire intelligible realm. Zorzi harmonises this Augustinian epistemology with the Hermetic ontology of Man: the soul knows all things because it *is* all things in attenuated form.

Boethius's image of the rational soul as a consonance of grave and acute voices — a musical metaphor drawn from his *De Institutione Musica* — brings the entire discourse into harmonic key. Boethius argues that the cosmos itself is constructed according to musical proportions (ratios of 2:1, 3:2, 4:3, etc.), and that the human soul, as the microcosmic image of the world-soul, must itself be constituted by these same proportions. To live harmoniously is thus to attune one's internal ratios to the cosmic harmonies. Discord, vice, and spiritual death result from the soul's falling out of tune with its own nature and the nature of the All.

A Zoroastrian oracle — "L'uomo esulta per l'armonia nella quale il corpo mortale sussiste" — is cited as corroborating testimony. This attribution to Zoroaster (whether genuine or apocryphal; such oracles circulated in late antique pseudo-Zoroastrian collections) provides a non-Abrahamic religious parallel to Zorzi's vision. The emphasis on "exultation" (exsultatio) in harmony suggests a soteriology: human flourishing, indeed human redemption, consists in the recovery or restoration of harmonic integrity. This moves the analysis beyond mere metaphysics into ethics and spirituality.

Origen's reading of "Maschio e femmina li creò" is adduced: the male principle signifies the intellect/divine element, the female the animal body, and their harmonious union is the task of human life. Origen's exegesis, particularly in his *Contra Celsum* and *Homilies on Genesis*, interprets the sexual distinction in Genesis 1:27 allegorically: the masculine refers to the rational, the eternal, the unchanging — the imago Dei in its highest aspect; the feminine refers to the bodily, the temporal, the mutable. This is not a misogynistic anthropology in Origen's mind but a diagram of the soul's internal structure and its existential task. The reintegration of masculine and feminine within the human being corresponds to the Neoplatonic ascent of the soul from body to intellect, and to the Christian path of theosis (deification) in which the human person becomes united to the divine nature while remaining fully human.

---

## Caput VI: "Con quale eleganza si ritrovano gli elementi nell'uomo"

The formal analogical system begins. The four elements are present in man with greater nobility than in any other composite being, because man alone among living creatures participates in celestial and divine life. Zorzi gives the classical Hippocratic-Galenic ratios of the humours: blood (8), phlegm (4), bile (2), black bile (1) — a series of double proportions which he identifies as constituting a musical harmony. He criticises Pietro d'Abano (the Conciliatore) for following Alexander of Aphrodisias in assigning superpartient ratios to the humours, on the Proclean principle that harmony cannot arise from superpartient proportions.

The selection of the 8:4:2:1 ratios (each term half the preceding) is far more than a medical observation. These ratios correspond to the tetrachord structure of ancient Greek music: the octave (2:1), the perfect fifth (3:2), the perfect fourth (4:3), and their subdivisions. By assigning them to the four humours in their bodily proportion, Zorzi is claiming that the physical constitution of the human body is literally musical. Health is harmony; disease is dissonance. This is the fundamental principle of *musica universalis* extended into physiology.

The criticism of Pietro d'Abano and Alexander of Aphrodisias reveals Zorzi's commitment to Neoplatonic mathematics over Aristotelian empiricism. Proclus, in his *Commentary on Plato's Timaeus*, argues that harmonic ratios in the cosmos must be *multiple* ratios (2:1, 3:1, 4:1) or *superparticular* ratios (3:2, 4:3, 5:4) — ratios in which the difference between the two terms is a simple unit fraction of the smaller term. Superpartient ratios (like 5:3, where the difference 2 is not a unit fraction of 3) were considered by Proclus to be discordant and therefore incapable of generating true harmony. Zorzi adopts this Proclean stricture as a principle of cosmic order: if the universe is harmonious (and it must be, because it emanates from the divine Intellect), then its fundamental ratios must obey Proclean harmonic law. Thus the medical observation that blood : phlegm : yellow bile : black bile :: 8 : 4 : 2 : 1 becomes theological evidence: the bodily constitution preserves in itself the harmonic structure of the cosmos.

---

## Caput VII: "Che cosa nell'uomo corrisponde agli elementi"

The five external senses correspond to the four elements: sight to fire; hearing to air; smell and taste to water; touch to earth. This mapping follows Augustine, though Zorzi notes that Francesco di Meyronnes (the "raffinatissimo Francesco") dissents on the assignment of taste to water.

This assignment of sensory faculties to the elemental powers is rooted in ancient pneumatic physiology. Fire, being the most rarefied and active element, was thought to be the vehicle of perception (aisthesis); it is the element most capable of conveying impressions swiftly. Air, being likewise subtle and pervasive, carries sound-vibrations. Water, being fluid and perceptive of chemical substances, corresponds to the taste and smell — the chemical senses. Earth, being solid and the seat of tactile sensation (the most fundamental and bodily of the senses), corresponds to touch. Augustine's *De Genesi ad litteram* and his *Confessiones* elaborate this schema in a Christian framework: the five senses are the five doors through which the soul encounters the sensible world and is either elevated toward the intelligible through contemplation, or degraded through lust and attachment to bodily pleasure.

Francesco di Meyronnes (or Franciscus de Mayronis), a fourteenth-century Franciscan theologian, refined Augustinian sensory theory through engagement with Aristotelian psychology. His dissent on the assignment of taste to water reflects a different view of taste's relationship to the material elements — possibly assigning it to earth (as the sense of direct contact with solid particles) or to a combination of elements. Zorzi's acknowledgment of this dissent, without fully endorsing it, shows his willingness to note genuine scholarly dispute while maintaining his own harmonic schema.

---

## Caput VIII: "A quali pietre corrispondono le parti del corpo"

Stones correspond to bones: both occupy the framework of their respective macrocosm/microcosm. Zorzi cites Empedocles' dictum that "stones are the bones of the world" and the Ovidian verse on Deucalion's stones. He defends a graduated notion of life in stones against Albert the Great's objection (that stones lack porosity for life to penetrate), arguing that life exists at many ontological levels — stones live their one operation (attracting, repelling, gladdening), just as angels, men, plants, and animals each live a different grade. The Psalms (Exultabunt omnia ossa mea; Conturbata sunt omnia ossa mea) are read as evidence that the "bones of the better man" — the spiritual man — also have their counterpart.

Empedocles' teaching (fr. B 71 Diels-Kranz) that the earth contains bones belonging to itself as a kind of skeletal structure represents an early hylozoistic vision of the cosmos as a living whole. This is transmitted through Neoplatonic sources, particularly Plotinus's *Enneads*, where the material cosmos is understood as animated throughout by the World-Soul. Ovid's Metamorphoses (1.393–415) recounts the myth of Deucalion and Pyrrha, who, after Zeus's deluge, repopulate the earth by casting behind them the "bones of the mother" (terrae ossa) — stones that transform into human beings. Zorzi reads this myth not as poetic fancy but as theological instruction: the entire cosmos, from stone to human, is organically unified as a living body.

Albert the Great's objection — that stones are inert and porous to any vital principle — represents the Aristotelian-Scholastic worry about panvitalism. How can a stone, lacking evident sense or motion, be said to "live"? Zorzi's response is elegant: life is not a single, univocal property but an analogical one. A stone "lives" by virtue of its essence, its operations, its attraction and repulsion. It performs its own operation perfectly (the operation proper to a stone). This follows the Aristotelian-Thomistic principle of *actus* and *potentia*: each being actualises its own potential in its own mode. A stone's actualisation of its stony nature is a kind of "life" — the only life a stone can have, but genuine nonetheless.

The Psalmic verses — "Exultabunt omnia ossa mea" (All my bones shall exult, Psalm 35:10) and "Conturbata sunt omnia ossa mea" (All my bones are troubled, Psalm 6:3) — are read as speaking of two different "men": the exterior man of flesh and bone, and the interior spiritual man. The "bones" of the spiritual man (his firmness, his foundational virtues, his unchanging essence in God) are the true bones that should exult. Zorzi is engaging in the allegorical exegesis that was standard in medieval monastic theology: the literal sense (historical or bodily) is true but inferior to the spiritual or mystical sense. Yet unlike many medieval exegetes, Zorzi does not abandon the literal sense; rather, he harmonises it with the spiritual. The literal bones of the body and the figurative bones of the soul are two manifestations of the same archetypal structure.

---

## Caput IX: "A che cosa corrispondono i metalli"

Bodily humours correspond to metals: both are aqueous in nature, both generated in the "womb" of earth or body under planetary influence. Zorzi synthesises Hermes (Evate, "king of the Arabs"), Ptolemy, Avicenna, Empedocles, Abubacher, and Albert on the generation of metals. Blood is identified as the seat of the soul (Leviticus: "Nel sangue dell'animale risiede la sua anima"), and Hermes Trismegistus is invoked for the doctrine that spirit dwells in blood as its vehicle.

The generation of metals was a central concern of both medieval alchemy and natural philosophy. Avicenna's *Kitab al-Shifa* (Metaphysics and Physics) and Albertus Magnus's *Summa de Creaturis* discuss how metals are formed within the earth through the action of heat and pneumatic forces (spiritual exhalations) acting upon base matter. The theory assumes that metals are not eternally fixed in their forms but can undergo transmutation and generation — a premise that alchemical art sought to accelerate and perfect. Zorzi places this cosmological theory into correspondence with the generation of humours in the body. Just as earth gives birth to metals through the agency of celestial heat and pneuma, so the body generates humours through the agency of the vital heat (calor innatus) and the spirit (spiritus). The four humours and the four metals are not merely analogous; they are homologous structures arising from the same cosmic principles operating at different scales.

The metals of the interior man then receive an extended allegorical-scriptural treatment: gold = charity (Apocalypse 3:18: "Ti consiglio di comprare da me dell'oro affinato con il fuoco"); silver = wisdom (purified "seven times," Psalm 12); bronze, tin, copper, iron, and lead = the baser moral states. The smelting imagery of Ezekiel, Isaiah, Malachi, and Jeremiah is mobilised to describe moral purification, with Pentecost fire as the eschatological furnace that fuses the community into "un cuore solo e un'anima sola" (Acts 4:32). The Hebrew urim ve-tummim (אוּרִים וְתֻמִּים), borne by the priests, is explained as "illuminazioni della verità e perfezioni di ogni genere" — a more satisfactory rendering than the Vulgate's "doctrina et veritas."

> *"Sedebit conflans, et emundans argentum, et purgabit filios Levi, qui praecipue purgati esse debent in auro charitatis, et argento sapientiae."*
<cite>p. 615 (Malachi 3:3, cited within Caput IX)</cite>

This passage from Malachi 3:3 — "He shall sit as a refiner and purifier of silver; and he shall purify the sons of Levi and refine them as gold and silver" — is interpreted as a prophecy of Christ the refiner who, through the eschatological fire, purifies the priestly people (the Church, the spiritual priesthood of all believers) in the furnace of tribulation. Gold here becomes the highest virtue, charity (agape), which is the form of all virtues. Silver becomes wisdom (sapientia), the intellectual apprehension of divine truth. Both are metals that survive the fire, unlike the base metals (tin, lead, copper, iron, bronze) which represent the passions, ignorance, and spiritual disorder.

The *urim ve-tummim* (literally "lights and perfections" or "lights and completions") were the priestly oracular device worn in the breastplate of the High Priest, described in Exodus 28:30 and mentioned in Leviticus 8:8 and 1 Samuel 28:6. In Jewish and Christian exegetical tradition, they were interpreted as divinely-granted means of discerning truth and making just decisions. Zorzi's reading — "illuminazioni della verità e perfezioni di ogni genere" (illuminations of truth and perfections of every kind) — goes beyond the Vulgate's more austere "doctrina et veritas" (teaching and truth). By rendering it as "perfezioni di ogni genere," Zorzi opens the symbol to encompass not merely intellectual knowledge but the full spectrum of virtues and perfections of the human being. The breastplate containing the *urim ve-tummim* becomes a symbol of the redeemed human heart, illuminated by divine truth and perfected in every faculty.

---

## Caput X: "A che cosa corrispondono alcuni minerali con mirabile artificio"

Mixed minerals — marcasite and alum — occupy the position between stones and metals, corresponding in the body to the intestines (which lie between bones and flesh). In the interior man, these correspond to the natural faculties: intellect, will, the irascible and concupiscible powers. Zorzi reads the plunder of the Temple by Antiochus (1 Maccabees) as an allegory of the devil's spoliation of these powers: the golden altar = will (seat of charity); the candelabrum = intellect; the golden incense-vessels = concupiscible power; the mortars = irascible power. The episode of Belshazzar's feast (Daniel 5) is adduced as a type of the abuse of these sacred vessels.

The placement of mixed minerals in an intermediate position reflects the alchemical and mineralogical taxonomy that Zorzi inherits. Marcasite (a form of iron sulfide, fool's gold) and alum (a compound salt with astringent and binding properties) are neither purely crystalline stones nor pure metallic elements. They represent a transitional category — the threshold between the purely material and the spiritually active. In the body, the intestines occupy a similar liminal space: they are the seat of the second digestion, where the chyme is further refined into chyle, where the transformation of gross matter into the subtle vehicle of nourishment occurs. Thus the intestines are the "laboratory" of the body, parallel to the alchemical vessel (athanor or furnace) in which base matter is refined.

The four natural faculties (intellect, will, irascible power, concupiscible power) derive from Aristotelian psychology as refined through medieval Scholasticism. The intellect (intellectus) is the faculty of understanding intelligible truths. The will (voluntas) is the faculty of appetite for the good as apprehended by intellect — the seat of love and charity. The irascible power (vis irascibilis) is the aggressive, defensive, spirited faculty that responds to obstacles and injustices. The concupiscible power (vis concupiscibilis) is the appetitive faculty drawn toward sensible goods and away from harms. In the fallen human being, these four are often in conflict; in the redeemed and virtuous person, they are harmonised under the rule of reason and grace.

Zorzi's reading of the desecration of the Temple by Antiochus IV Epiphanes (as recounted in 1 Maccabees 1:21–64) as an allegory of spiritual spoliation follows the typological exegetical tradition. The golden altar of incense = the will perfected in charity, because incense (the sweet odor ascending to heaven) is a symbol of prayer and of love's ascent to God. The candelabrum (menorah) with its seven branches = the intellect illuminated by the sevenfold gifts of the Holy Spirit, or the intellect's apprehension of the intelligible world structured in hierarchical degrees. The golden incense-vessels = the concupiscible power, which must be refined and turned toward spiritual goods rather than base pleasures. The mortars (for pounding and mixing the incense) = the irascible power, which must be tempered and directed toward righteous indignation against vice rather than mere anger.

Belshazzar's feast (Daniel 5), in which the Babylonian king profanes the holy vessels taken from the Jerusalem Temple by drinking wine from them and praising his idols, becomes a type of the soul's degradation when the sacred powers are prostituted to base ends. To use the vessels of the Temple for carnal revelry is to corrupt and invert their function — just as to use intellect for sophistry and deception, or will for selfish aggrandisement, or the irascible power for cruelty, or the concupiscible power for gluttony and lust, is to pervert the soul's native nobility.

---

## Caput XI: "Insieme a quali parti del corpo le piante vegetano e verdeggiano"

Vegetative life in the body is found in flesh, blood, and vital spirits. Aristotle's image of man as an "inverted tree" (roots = hair, nourishment taken from above through the mouth) is set alongside the Psalmic "trees of Adonay" (cedars of Lebanon) as figures of celestial plants. The planting of paradise (Genesis 2:8) is etymologised: the Hebrew מקדם (michedem, "from the east" or "from the beginning") is read so that chedem suggests the Father as root and principle of all trees. The chapter ends with the Johannine prayer (John 17:21, "Rogo Pater, ut sicut ego et tu unum sumus") as the telos of the plant-image: the human tree, rooted in the heavenly planter, returns to unity with its source.

> *"Plantat enim has arbores ille, de quo dicitur: Omnis plantatio, quam non plantavit Pater meus coelestis, eradicabitur."*
<cite>p. 622 (Caput XI)</cite>

The image of man as an inverted tree appears in the *Timaeus* of Plato (90a), where the head is said to be the "root" suspended from the celestial cosmos, while the roots of plants extend downward into the earth. This inversion signals the difference between celestial and sublunary nature: the celestial always tends upward toward the fixed stars and the intelligible realm; the terrestrial and bodily always tends downward. But in man, the inversion is overcome. The human being is an inverted tree whose roots reach toward heaven, whose nourishment descends from above through the mouth (intellect receives intelligible nourishment through contemplation), and whose fruits grow upward toward the divine. This is the cosmic orientation proper to the microcosm.

The reference to the "cedars of Adonay" (אַרְזֵי אֱדֹנָי, arazei Adonay, Psalm 104:16) applies a botanical metaphor to the celestial powers and intelligences. Cedar is the noblest of trees, imperishable and fragrant, used in the construction of the Temple. To call the angelic powers "trees of the Lord" is to assert their rootedness in the divine nature and their function as conduits of divine life into the cosmos. The human being, in turn, is called to become a "tree planted by streams of water" (Psalm 1:3), irrigated by the waters of grace and bearing fruit in its season.

The etymological reading of *michedem* (מקדם) as containing *chedem* (קדם, the East or Antiquity, but also by extension the primal, the archetypal, and the fatherly) is a Kabbalistic technique. It suggests that the garden of Eden, planted "in the beginning" by God, is planted in God the Father as its eternal root and source. The Father is qedem, the Primordial, the Transcendent One from whom all creatures and all gardens proceed. Thus to be planted in Eden is to be ultimately rooted in the Father's infinite generative power.

The conclusion with the Johannine prayer — "Rogo Pater, ut sicut ego et tu unum sumus" (I pray, Father, that as you and I are one) — brings the botanical metaphor into the most intimate mystical space. The prayer, spoken by Christ on the eve of His Passion, petitions that the disciples (and by extension, all believers) be brought into the unity of love that exists between the Father and the Son. The vegetative life of man, rooted in the Father, nourished by the divine grace, bearing fruits of virtue and love, finds its ultimate purpose in theosis — union with God through love. The tree of the garden of Eden, the tree of human nature, the human being as microcosm — all are ordered toward a single end: participation in the eternal life and eternal unity of the divine Trinity.

---

## Caput XII–XIII: Animal Life and Metempsychosis

Chapter XII establishes that animal faculties — sensation, movement, instinct — are present in man, with each species of animal corresponding to a faculty or bodily member. Chapter XIII addresses metempsychosis at length, reviewing the positions of the major ancient schools: Xenocrates, Arcesilaus, Carneades, Ammonius on how to read Plato's transmigration myths; Iamblichus and Porphyry who reject literal transmigration of rational souls into beasts; Proclus, Hermias, and Syrianus who find it monstrous. Zorzi's conclusion follows Porphyry: a morally depraved man is not an ass but "asinino" — having the quality of an ass. This sets up the positive doctrine: what the ancients expressed mythologically through metamorphosis, the sacred authors express as the spiritual transformation of the interior man.

The doctrine of metempsychosis or palingenesia (reincarnation) appears in Plato's *Republic* (620a), *Phaedrus* (248–249), and *Timaeus* (91–92) as a framework for understanding divine justice and the soul's education. Xenocrates, Arcesilaus, and Carneades (representing the Old and Middle Academy and the Skeptical Academy respectively) debated whether Plato meant the doctrine literally or allegorically. Iamblichus and Porphyry, as Neoplatonists, rejected the notion that the rational soul (*nous*) could genuinely inhabit an animal body; the rational soul is by nature eternal and immaterial and cannot be imprisoned in a beast's form. Rather, *irrational* souls (the vegetative and sensitive souls that animate plants and animals) are what transmigrate, while the rational soul remains in the intelligible realm, linked to the incarnate human being through a kind of attention or inclination (*proclivity*).

Proclus, Hermias, and Syrianus — the heads and commentators of the Neoplatonic school in the fifth century CE — found literal metempsychosis into beasts philosophically monstrous. How could the rational soul, eternal and infinite, be degraded into a finite, temporal, bestial form? Their resolution: what Plato expresses through the myth of reincarnation is the eternal truth that there are eternally many grades of being, from God down through intelligences, rational souls, sensitive souls, and plants. The soul that appears to be reincarnated in an animal is not the rational soul of a fallen human being but an eternally existing sensitive soul, or perhaps a representation and consequence of that soul's choices in a prior human life.

Zorzi follows Porphyry's moral reading: when a human being becomes bestial in character — pursuing only sensual pleasures, lacking reason, operating from rage or appetite — he does not literally become a donkey, but he becomes "asinino," ass-like, asinine. The rational soul remains rational, but it is disfigured and enslaved by vice; it operates at the level of the animal powers; it has lost its humanity in all but the external form. This reading preserves the Neoplatonic metaphysical principle that the rational soul cannot truly become irrational, while affirming the profound moral insight that vice genuinely degrades the human being toward bestiality.

Zorzi then transposes this doctrine into the language of Christian anthropology and spiritual transformation. The mythological language of metempsychosis, used by the ancients, expressed the truth that the soul undergoes radical metamorphosis through sin and grace, through vice and virtue. Sacred scripture, in its more direct and less veiled speech, teaches the same truth: "Unless you become like little children, you shall not enter the kingdom of heaven" (Matthew 18:3); "Put off the old man with his deeds and put on the new man" (Colossians 3:9–10); "I live, yet not I, but Christ lives in me" (Galatians 2:20). The spiritual transformation of the interior man — from bondage to passion to freedom in grace, from ignorance to wisdom, from death to life — is the Christian equivalent of the soul's transmigration and redemption that the ancient mysteries taught in mythic form.

---

## Capp. XIV–XVI: Noah's Ark as Cosmic Image

These chapters interpret Noah's ark (Genesis 6–7) as a comprehensive image of man containing all species within himself. The prescription of seven pairs of clean animals and one pair of unclean (Genesis 7:2) receives a numerological treatment: seven corresponds to the superior goods, one to the material.

The ark becomes, in Zorzi's reading, a microcosmic vessel containing the cosmos. It is a "box" or "house" (the Hebrew תֵּבָה *tebah*) fashioned according to precise divine proportion and measurement, just as the cosmos itself is proportioned and the human body is proportioned. The ark's inhabitants — the pairs of animals, the vegetation, the human family — represent the totality of creation preserved and renewed. The flood itself represents a cosmic dissolution and regeneration, parallel to the mystical death and resurrection that the human being undergoes in spiritual transformation. 

The numerological reading of seven and one — seven pairs of clean (ritually pure) animals and one pair of unclean — encodes a cosmological truth about the hierarchy of being. Seven is the number of the planets, the heavens, the septenary structure of creation under the governance of divine Providence. One is the monad, the material principle, the singular, the earthly. The preservation of seven pairs of clean animals speaks to the preservation of the celestial and spiritual powers, their sevenfold variety, their plural manifestations. The preservation of only one pair of unclean animals speaks to the reduced but necessary presence of the material principle. Thus the ark's manifest structure is also a teaching about the proportions and harmonies that sustain creation.

---

## Capp. XVII–XXIX: Celestial Correspondences

The longest section maps the heavens onto man. Chapter XVII argues that all the heavens are contained in the human being. Chapters XVIII–XIX treat the zodiacal signs and their distribution over twelve members of the body (duodenary number). Chapter XX–XXIX constitute a planet-by-planet analysis: each planet corresponds to specific faculties, qualities, and bodily regions — Saturn to condensation and fixity, Jupiter to balance and governance, Mars to heat and irascibility, the Sun to vivification and illumination, Venus to softening and love, Mercury to acuity and penetration, the Moon to fluidity and change. Chapter XXX draws the conclusion that the proportional motions of the planets among themselves and relative to the eighth sphere mirror the proportions of our interior forces.

The planetary correspondences constitute the heart of Renaissance astrological anthropology and are grounded in ancient medical and philosophical sources. In the *Tetrabiblos* (Four Books), Ptolemy describes how the planets, through their conjunctions, aspects, and transits, shape the temperament and fortune of human beings born under their influence. The medieval medical tradition, particularly in Avicenna's *Canon*, elaborates a complex system of planetary rulerships over bodily organs and functions. Saturn rules the bones and the spleen (melancholy, contraction, limitation); Jupiter rules the liver and the circulation of blood (expansion, growth, generosity); Mars rules the gall bladder and muscular action (heat, aggression, assertion); the Sun rules the heart and the vital spirit (illumination, sovereignty, life-force); Venus rules the kidneys and reproductive power (softening, harmony, pleasure); Mercury rules the lungs and the nervous system (communication, differentiation, acuity); the Moon rules the brain and the fluid system (imagination, changeability, receptivity).

But Zorzi goes further. He is not merely assigning planetary rulerships in a mechanical, astrological sense. Rather, he is arguing that the planetary powers themselves are intelligences, angelic or divine forces that manifest through the celestial bodies and their cycles. The planets are not mere mechanisms but living, intelligent agents expressing divine archetypes. When Mars acts upon the human being, it is the archangelic power associated with Mars — the power of righteous assertion and divine indignation — that is operative. Similarly for all the planets. Thus the human being who contemplates the planetary correspondences within himself is not merely acquiring astrological knowledge but entering into a theurgic awareness of the divine powers that constitute and animate his being.

The zodiacal distribution over twelve bodily members invokes another layer of correspondence. The twelve signs of the zodiac (Aries, Taurus, Gemini, etc.) are distributed over the twelve main members or regions of the body: Aries rules the head, Taurus the neck, Gemini the arms, Cancer the breast, Leo the heart, Virgo the belly, Libra the loins, Scorpio the genitalia, Sagittarius the thighs, Capricorn the knees, Aquarius the shins, Pisces the feet. This distribution appears in ancient medical and astrological texts (e.g., in the *Iatromathematica* of Hephaestion of Thebes) and becomes standard in medieval and Renaissance medicine. The twelve thus corresponds to the twelve tribes of Israel (who receive different blessings from Jacob in Genesis 49), the twelve stones of the priestly breastplate (Exodus 28), the twelve gates of the New Jerusalem (Revelation 21), and the twelve apostles of Christ. All these twelves are expressions of the same archetypal structure: the complete, harmonious organization of the cosmos reflected in human form.

The proportional motions of the planets — their synodic periods (conjunction to conjunction), their relative speeds, their aspect patterns — are mathematically precise and expressible as ratios. The conjunction of Saturn and Jupiter, for example, repeats approximately every 20 years, and the conjunctions precess through the zodiacal signs in a great cycle of approximately 800 years. These ratios are the very ratios that appear in the *De Harmonia Mundi*'s harmonic system. Thus Zorzi is claiming that the macrocosmic celestial machinery and the microcosmic human being operate according to identical mathematical principles. To understand the harmony of the heavens is to understand one's own internal constitution.

---

## Capp. XXXI–XXXIV: Angels, God, and the Divine Members

The final movement of the section treats the highest correspondences. Chapter XXXI addresses the harmony of disordered things and spiritual "scoria." Chapter XXXII argues that man contains the angels within himself and harmonises with them. Chapter XXXIII treats man as "temple and image of God" singing in concert with God. Chapter XXXIV — "De consonantia membrorum Dei et hominis" — is the climax: Zorzi surveys the scriptural "members" attributed to God (eye, hand, mouth, arm, etc.) as analogical figures of the divine nature, correlating them with human faculties. The Trinitarian structure is mapped via Hermes Trismegistus ("Il Padre di tutte le cose essendo intelletto, vita e splendore, generò l'uomo simile a sé") onto man's intellect, life, and love. The garden of Eden with its Tree of Life (= the Father as source of all rational creatures) and Tree of Knowledge of Good and Evil (= Christ, the only being simultaneously *beatus* and *viator*) provides the final Kabbalistic-arboreal image.

The ascent from the material elements, through stones and plants and animals, through the celestial spheres, through the angels, and finally to God represents the Neoplatonic scala perfectionis — the ladder of being upon which the soul ascends toward the Absolute. But it is also, paradoxically, a descent: for Zorzi maintains that all these levels are *already present* in the human microcosm. To know oneself, in Zorzi's vision, is to trace this entire descent and ascent, to actualise the potential of one's nature by becoming consciously aware of all the grades of being that one already contains.

The "harmony of disordered things" in Chapter XXXI suggests that even chaos, discord, and apparent evil have a place in the cosmos's overall harmony. This is a deeply Neoplatonic thought, influenced by Plotinus's explanation of evil not as an absolute principle but as the necessary "matter" or privation that is the inevitable limit of emanation. Just as in music, dissonances can be employed artfully to heighten the effect of consonances, so in the cosmos, the disorders and sufferings of the material world serve ultimately to make the goodness and beauty of the intelligible realm shine more brilliantly.

Chapter XXXII on man's harmony with the angels introduces the doctrine that the human being's rational soul is itself a kind of angel, or at least shares the nature of angels. The human intellect, in its highest operations, is in direct communion with the angelic intelligences. When a human being acts from reason and virtue, he aligns himself with the angelic order; when he acts from passion and vice, he misaligns himself. The angels are the cosmic powers that mediate between the divine unity and the multiplicity of creation; the human being, as a microcosm, performs a similar mediation in miniature — reconciling the divine spark within with the material and sensory world without.

Chapter XXXIII's image of man as "temple and image of God" invokes 1 Corinthians 6:19 ("Know you not that your bodies are the temples of the Holy Ghost?") and 1 Corinthians 11:7 ("Man is the image and glory of God"). The human being is the place where God dwells, the sanctum where the holy of holies resides. This is not metaphorical merely; it is ontologically true. The human being, precisely as microcosm, as the recapitulation of all creation, is the cosmic temple in which the divine presence is housed. To sing "in concert with God" is to align one's will, intellect, and every faculty with the divine will and mind, becoming an instrument through which the divine music resounds in the material world.

Chapter XXXIV's treatment of the divine "members" (*membra Dei*) is the culmination. Scriptural language often attributes to God human members: God's eye sees all; God's hand created and sustains; God's mouth speaks the word that creates; God's arm redeems and protects. These are anthropomorphisms, yes — but for Zorzi (following the Neoplatonic and Kabbalistic exegetical tradition), they are not merely figurative. They express eternal truths about the divine nature refracted through human categories of understanding. The human being, made in God's image, contains within himself archetypal forms of all these divine "members." The human eye, when purified and illuminated, becomes an expression of God's vision; the human hand, when moved by charity, becomes an expression of God's creative and sustaining power. The human being, in achieving spiritual perfection, becomes a conscious, living manifestation of the divine archetypes.

> *"Homo est quoddam omne, et quoddam totum in omni, id est in Deo, qui omnia virtute constat, non quidem compositione, homo autem compositione constans est ex omnibus rerum potestatibus."*
<cite>p. 603 (Hermes Trismegistus, cited in Caput V)</cite>

This quotation from the *Corpus Hermeticum* (likely *Asclepius* 6–7) encapsulates the entire doctrine. Man is "quoddam omne" — a certain whole, a whole that is self-contained and complete in its own order. He is "quoddam totum in omni" — a whole that participates in the All, that mirrors the All. And above all, he mirrors God, *qui omnia virtute constat* — God who holds all things in virtue (potency, power, actuality), not through composition or mixture (God is utterly simple), while man, being composite and material, actualises his potential through the composition of all the powers of things. Thus man's composite multiplicity images God's infinite simplicity; man's temporal becoming images God's eternal being; man's partial knowledge images God's infinite knowing. The harmonic structures that rule the cosmos — from the proportions of the heavens to the ratios of the humours — are the visible signatures of the invisible reality of God, and the human being who understands these harmonies comes to understand himself, and in understanding himself, comes to understand God.

---

## Zorzi's Method and Sources

Throughout, Zorzi's method is the harmonisation of multiple traditions: Kabbalistic letter-hermeneutics (the name Adam, urim ve-tummim), Neoplatonic hierarchies (Proclus on superpartient proportions, Porphyry and Iamblichus on the soul), Hermetic anthropology (Trismegistus on man as "totum in omni"), Aristotelian natural philosophy, Galenic medicine, and scriptural typology. The figures of the Tabernacle and Temple, Noah's Ark, the metals offered for the Temple's construction, and the garden of Eden all function as divinely encoded diagrams of the microcosm. The section as a whole is the most extended and systematic anthropological statement in the *De Harmonia Mundi*, and constitutes Zorzi's fullest elaboration of the claim that "harmonia" is not merely a cosmological property but the very constitution of the human person. In Zorzi's vision, to live in harmony is to actualise one's human nature as a microcosm, to consciously align all the levels of one's being — material, vital, sensitive, rational, and divine — in a unified ascent toward God. The human being is not a merely accidental assemblage of body and soul, matter and spirit, but a deliberate, artful, and divinely-proportioned image of the entire cosmos, fashioned by the supreme architect as a bridge between the eternal and the temporal, the infinite and the finite, the divine and the created.
